expanded_description: The seven quadrilateral types studied in this unit form a nested hierarchy in which each type inherits all properties of the types above it and adds its own distinguishing features. A square is a rhombus and a rectangle. A rhombus and a rectangle are both parallelograms. Parallelograms are trapezoids under the inclusive definition. Understanding this family tree transforms a collection of separate facts into a coherent logical structure. Properties flow downward—prove a shape belongs at one level, and all the properties of its ancestors come for free.

core_concept.principle: The quadrilateral family tree organizes shapes by their defining properties. At the root is the general quadrilateral. One branch leads to trapezoids (at least one pair of parallel sides), which contains parallelograms (two pairs). Parallelograms branch into rectangles (four right angles) and rhombuses (four congruent sides). Their intersection is the square. A separate branch contains kites. The isosceles trapezoid sits under the trapezoid branch. Every property assigned to a parent type is automatically true for all its descendants.
core_concept.explanation: Imagine the quadrilateral family as an inheritance tree, like a family passing down heirlooms. The original ancestor, Quadrilateral, passes down one heirloom: the sum of interior angles is 360°. Trapezoid inherits this and adds: at least one pair of parallel sides. Parallelogram inherits everything from its ancestors and adds: both pairs of opposite sides parallel, opposite sides congruent, opposite angles congruent, consecutive angles supplementary, and diagonals that bisect each other. Rectangle takes all of that and adds: four right angles and congruent diagonals. Rhombus takes the Parallelogram inheritance and adds: four congruent sides, perpendicular diagonals, and angle-bisecting diagonals. Square, as the child of both Rectangle and Rhombus, gets everything—it is the wealthiest member of the family, possessing every listed property. Isosceles Trapezoid inherits from Trapezoid and adds congruent legs, base angles, and diagonals. Kite stands on a separate branch, inheriting only the 360° sum and adding its own unique properties. This inheritance structure means that proving a shape is, say, a rhombus instantly grants you access to all parallelogram and quadrilateral properties without needing to prove them individually.

image.alt: Family tree diagram. Root: Quadrilateral. Branches: Kite and Trapezoid. From Trapezoid: Isosceles Trapezoid and Parallelogram. From Parallelogram: Rectangle and Rhombus. These merge at Square. Each node displays its defining properties. Arrows show inheritance direction—properties flow downward.
image.caption: The Quadrilateral Family Tree: properties flow downward from ancestors to descendants.

sections.introduction: The quadrilateral family tree is a logical map. Every arrow represents a conditional statement: 'If a shape is a parallelogram, then it is a trapezoid.' 'If a shape is a square, then it is a rectangle.' Following these arrows reveals the complete set of properties any quadrilateral possesses based on its most specific classification.

introduction_image.alt: Family tree with each node labeled and connected. Sidebar: 'To find all properties of a square, trace upward—square inherits from rhombus, rectangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, and quadrilateral.'
introduction_image.caption: The family tree: trace upward from any node to collect all inherited properties.

key_concept.definition: The hierarchy (most general to most specific): Quadrilateral → Trapezoid (at least one pair parallel sides) → Parallelogram (both pairs parallel). Parallelogram → Rectangle (four right angles). Parallelogram → Rhombus (four congruent sides). Rectangle + Rhombus → Square. Trapezoid → Isosceles Trapezoid (congruent legs). Separate branch: Kite (two pairs adjacent congruent sides). Each arrow means 'is a type of.' A square is a type of rhombus and a type of rectangle. A rectangle is a type of parallelogram. A parallelogram is a type of trapezoid (under the inclusive definition). All are types of quadrilaterals.
key_concept.context: This hierarchy uses the inclusive definition of trapezoid (at least one pair of parallel sides). Under this definition, every parallelogram qualifies as a trapezoid. The inclusive definition produces the cleanest hierarchy. Property inheritance works regardless of the definition chosen for trapezoid.

image.alt: Complete family tree with inclusive definition noted. Smaller alternative tree showing exclusive definition for comparison.
image.caption: The complete hierarchy with inheritance rules.

process_flow.process: Place the Quadrilateral at Its Most Specific Node
process_flow.description: Given a quadrilateral's properties, identify its most precise classification—the lowest point it occupies in the tree.
process_flow.mechanism: Start from the top. Does it have at least one pair of parallel sides? If no, check for kite properties. If neither, general quadrilateral. If yes to one pair, at least a trapezoid. Two pairs? Parallelogram. Four right angles? Rectangle. Four congruent sides? Rhombus. Both? Square. For a trapezoid, congruent legs? Isosceles trapezoid.
applications: Four right angles + four congruent sides → Square.
Four congruent sides, no right angles → Rhombus.

image.alt: Flowchart from Quadrilateral through decision points to each terminal classification.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the most specific classification using the tree.

process_flow.process: Trace Upward to Collect All Inherited Properties
process_flow.description: From the most specific node, follow the tree upward to the root. Every property listed at any ancestor node applies.
process_flow.mechanism: For a square: trace up to rhombus (four congruent sides, perpendicular diagonals, angle-bisecting diagonals), to rectangle (four right angles, congruent diagonals), to parallelogram (both pairs parallel, opposite sides and angles congruent, consecutive supplementary, diagonals bisect), to trapezoid (midsegment formula), to quadrilateral (360° sum). The square possesses all of these. For a kite: trace only to quadrilateral—kites do not inherit parallelogram or trapezoid properties.
process_flow.result: Complete property set for the quadrilateral.
applications: A rhombus has perpendicular diagonals (its own), but NOT congruent diagonals (does not pass through rectangle).
A rectangle has congruent diagonals (its own), but NOT perpendicular diagonals (does not pass through rhombus).

image.alt: Tree with square highlighted. Dashed lines trace upward through all ancestors. Cumulative property list builds on the side.
image.caption: Step 2: Trace upward—every ancestor's properties are inherited.

classifications.value: A comprehensive matrix showing every property and which quadrilateral types possess it.
characteristics: Rows: seven quadrilateral types plus general quadrilateral.

Columns: every property studied in Unit 6—interior sum 360°, parallel sides, congruent sides, congruent angles, supplementary angles, bisecting diagonals, congruent diagonals, perpendicular diagonals, angle-bisecting diagonals, midsegment formula, congruent legs, congruent base angles.
The pattern visually encodes the entire hierarchy—squares have every checkmark, rhombuses and rectangles share the parallelogram row, kites have a sparse distinctive pattern.

examples.explanation: A square has checkmarks in all twelve columns. A rhombus has all except the rectangle-specific ones (congruent diagonals, all angles right). A rectangle has all except the rhombus-specific ones (perpendicular diagonals, angle-bisecting diagonals, all sides congruent). The table makes inheritance visible at a glance.

image.alt: Table with quadrilateral types as rows and properties as columns. Checkmarks showing inheritance pattern.
image.caption: Property inheritance table: a visual representation of the entire hierarchy.

image.alt: Complete family tree, property inheritance table, and Venn diagram displayed together.
image.caption: Quadrilateral Family Tree overview.

sections.introduction: Apply the family tree to classify quadrilaterals, determine inherited properties, and understand set relationships.

worked_examples.title: Family Tree Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Four congruent sides, perpendicular diagonals. Most specific classification?
2) Both pairs parallel, four right angles, diagonals congruent but not perpendicular. Classify.
3) List every property a square possesses by being a square.
4) True/False: All rectangles are squares. Explain using the tree.
5) Under the inclusive definition, explain why every parallelogram is a trapezoid.

step_by_step.explanation: Four congruent sides → rhombus. Perpendicular diagonals confirm. No right angles mentioned → not square. Rhombus.
Both pairs parallel → parallelogram. Four right angles → rectangle. Diagonals congruent confirms. Not perpendicular → not rhombus, not square. Rectangle.

360° sum (quadrilateral). At least one pair parallel (trapezoid). Both pairs parallel, opposite sides ≅, opposite angles ≅, consecutive supplementary, diagonals bisect (parallelogram). Four right angles, diagonals ≅ (rectangle). Four sides ≅, diagonals ⟂, diagonals bisect angles (rhombus). All twelve properties.

Rectangle and Rhombus are separate branches under Parallelogram. Square is their intersection. A shape is a square only if it satisfies BOTH branches. A rectangle satisfies only the rectangle branch. It must also have four congruent sides to be a square.

Inclusive trapezoid: at least one pair of parallel sides. A parallelogram has two pairs, which satisfies 'at least one.' Therefore every parallelogram is a trapezoid. Parallelograms are a subset of trapezoids.

image.alt: Five panels with diagrams and reasoning.
image.caption: Family tree review problems.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Rhombus.
2) Rectangle.
3) All twelve listed properties.
4) False—rectangles need four congruent sides to be squares.
5) Parallelograms satisfy 'at least one pair' of parallel sides.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Quadrilateral hierarchy, property inheritance, and set relationships.

summary: The quadrilateral family tree organizes seven types into a nested hierarchy. Each type inherits all properties of its ancestors and adds its own. A square inherits from rhombus, rectangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, and quadrilateral—possessing twelve distinct geometric properties simply by being a square. The hierarchy transforms separate facts into a logical structure where proving a shape's classification automatically grants all ancestor properties. The property inheritance table and Venn diagram provide complementary visual representations of these relationships.

review_questions_image.alt: Complete family tree with all properties listed at each level, displayed as a comprehensive reference poster.
review_questions_image.caption: Review of Quadrilateral Family Tree.
